title: OTC medicines by active ingredient
summary: Choose one active ingredient for one target symptom whenever possible. Multi-symptom products often add ingredients you do not need and make accidental double-dosing more likely.
evidenceSnapshot: Analgesics have the clearest benefit. Pseudoephedrine and topical decongestants can help short-term congestion; oral phenylephrine efficacy is poor. Evidence for cough suppressants/expectorants is inconsistent.

interventions.name: Paracetamol
interventions.practical: Adults/12+: no more than 1 g every 4–6 hours and 4 g in 24 hours unless a lower limit is advised.
interventions.cautions: Liver toxicity with overdose; check combinations.

interventions.name: Ibuprofen
interventions.finding: Effective for pain, fever and inflammatory discomfort.
interventions.practical: Follow the pack; use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time.
interventions.cautions: Caution with ulcers, kidney disease, dehydration, anticoagulants, cardiovascular disease, pregnancy and some asthma.

interventions.name: Pseudoephedrine
interventions.finding: Provides short-term relief of nasal congestion for many adults.
interventions.practical: Australian Schedule 3/4 medicine; discuss with a pharmacist.
interventions.cautions: May increase heart rate/BP, anxiety, tremor, insomnia or urinary retention.

interventions.name: Oral phenylephrine
interventions.finding: FDA evidence review found marketed oral doses ineffective for congestion.
interventions.mechanism: Low systemic exposure after oral use.
interventions.cautions: May still cause sympathomimetic side effects despite little benefit.

interventions.name: Oxymetazoline/xylometazoline nasal spray
interventions.practical: Use for only a few days according to the label.

interventions.name: Sedating first-generation antihistamines
interventions.finding: May slightly reduce sneezing/runny nose in adults but do not treat the virus and cause sedation/anticholinergic effects.
interventions.practical: Reserve for a clear reason and ask a pharmacist if older, taking other sedatives or driving.
interventions.cautions: Do not use for cough/cold/flu under 6; never under 2 for any indication.

interventions.name: Non-sedating antihistamines
interventions.finding: Useful for allergy, not generally for an uncomplicated viral cold.
interventions.practical: Use when symptoms fit allergy: itch, repeated sneezing, watery eyes, exposure pattern.
interventions.cautions: Can still cause side effects and does not shorten a cold.

interventions.name: Dextromethorphan
interventions.finding: May suppress a troublesome dry cough in some adults; results are inconsistent.
interventions.practical: Follow label; avoid duplicate cough products.

interventions.name: Guaifenesin
interventions.finding: May help expectoration in some people but evidence is limited.
interventions.practical: Use with fluids and follow label.
interventions.cautions: Do not delay review for breathlessness or blood.

interventions.name: Combination cold-and-flu products
interventions.finding: May offer small multi-symptom relief in adults/older children but increase side effects and dosing complexity.
interventions.practical: Write down every active ingredient and time taken.
interventions.cautions: High risk of duplicate paracetamol, sedatives or decongestants.

practicalSteps: List the symptoms that genuinely need treatment.
Read the active ingredients, not only the brand name.
Avoid taking two products containing paracetamol, antihistamine or decongestant.
Use a pharmacist for pregnancy, children, chronic illness or multiple regular medicines.
avoidOrCare: Do not use adult combination products in children.
Avoid sedating products before driving or caring for children overnight if you cannot wake reliably.
Do not exceed label duration for nasal decongestants.

myths.claim: A stronger multi-symptom product treats the infection better.
myths.reality: It only combines symptom medicines and may expose you to unnecessary ingredients.
Pseudoephedrine has better evidence than oral phenylephrine; nasal sprays work locally but require strict duration limits.

redFlags: Suspected overdose or duplicate paracetamol — call the Poisons Information Centre in Australia on 13 11 26
Palpitations, severe headache, chest pain or marked agitation after a decongestant
Allergic reaction, confusion or breathing difficulty
unsettled: Evidence for many older OTC cough products is dated, heterogeneous and formulation-specific.
